card_title: 3 ways CNY actually reaches your supplier

videos.video_title: The 3 paths your CNY takes to China (most operators only know one)

hook_options: A business owner sends CNY to their Chinese factory and waits 5 days.
Why does the same CNY payment land in 5 days for one business owner and same-day for another?
There are three different ways CNY reaches a Chinese supplier. Most business owners only know one.
Your wire confirmation says sent. Your supplier says nothing has arrived. The path your money is on decides who is right.
Three paths. Same CNY. One arrives in hours, one in days, one gets stuck.
variants.body: Her wire went through the international bank network, the slow path. Money hops bank to bank for 3 to 5 days. Meanwhile her factory waits to start production. There is a second path that clears in 1 to 2 days, China's own clearing system. And a third path designed for paying suppliers on 1688, the wholesale platform. That one arrives same-day. Her bank put her on the slow path by default and never told her there were other options. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same CNY payment, same supplier, but on the fastest path. Most land same-day.
audience_feedback: First line OK. Lady sending money and waiting 5 days. That is me. I wait also.
Wait, 'international bank network' is what? You mean my bank? I get confused.
I like the part about factory waiting to make my stuff. That is real. My factory always say wait wait wait.
Three paths sound like too many. I just want my money to go. I don't care about names.
End is weak. 'Check out WorldFirst.' Sound like every other ad. I scroll already.

hook_options: Three CNY paths to China. 3 to 5 days, 1 to 2 days, same-day.
Why does one CNY payment take 5 days and the next one takes 5 hours?
Your CNY can travel three different ways to China. Speed difference: 5x.
Your bank says CNY transfer. Your supplier says no money. Both true, because there are three different transfer types.
5 days. 2 days. Same-day. Three paths, same destination, same money.
variants.body: Path one, the global bank network. 3 to 5 business days. Money hops through 1 to 3 banks in the middle, each takes a fee, each takes a day. Path two, China's direct clearing system. 1 to 2 days, fewer middlemen. Path three, the rail built for paying 1688 suppliers. Around 80% land same-day, 90% by the next day. Same CNY amount, same supplier, three speeds. Most banks put you on path one by default because they make the most money on it. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst goes direct on the fast path.
audience_feedback: 'Speed difference 5x' I don't know what that mean. Five times? Too math.
Numbers come too fast. 3 to 5, 1 to 2, 80%, 90%. I cannot keep up.
I like 'banks make the most money on it'. So they cheat me? OK now I listen.
'Rail built for paying 1688 suppliers' is good. I use 1688. That speak to me.
Same ending. Search WorldFirst. Lazy. Tell me what I get when I search lah.

hook_options: Your bank tells you there is one way to send CNY to China. There are three.
Why is your CNY taking 5 days when CNY clears inside China in seconds?
Your bank picks the slowest CNY path on purpose. Here is why.
Everyone tells you a CNY wire is a CNY wire. Three different mechanisms move that money.
The CNY in China moves instantly. The CNY you send from outside takes 5 days. Why?
variants.body: Inside China, CNY moves between bank accounts in seconds. Outside, your CNY wire takes 3 to 5 days. The reason: your bank puts your money on the global international network, which routes through 2 or 3 middleman banks. There are two faster paths. China's direct clearing system, 1 to 2 days. And the rail used for paying 1688 suppliers, same-day. Your bank does not offer those because they do not own them. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends your CNY on the fast paths the banks do not show you.
audience_feedback: Good hook. My bank only tell me one way? Make me angry a bit. I want to know more.
'Clearing system' I don't know what is. Sound boring.
'Inside China seconds, outside 5 days' is good. That make sense to me, simple compare.
'Your bank does not offer because they do not own them' OK so the bank is hiding things from me. I believe.
Maybe I search. Maybe. I am tired now and got two kids.

card_title: Your supplier asked for the $200 they didn't receive

videos.video_title: Your supplier just asked for the $200 they did not get

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst (internal: World Account + MYbank, OUR routing)
hook_options: A business owner sends $50,000 to their Chinese factory. Supplier replies: we only got $49,800.
Why is your supplier asking for the $200 they say did not arrive?
$50,000 leaves your account. $49,800 arrives. The $200 just vanishes.
Your bank says sent in full. Your supplier says short payment. Both are telling the truth.
A business owner just got a message from her factory: short by $200. She wires the same amount the next day. Short again.
variants.body: Here is what happened. Her wire went through the international bank network. Money does not fly straight to China. It passes through 1 to 3 banks in the middle. Each one quietly takes a fee, usually $15 to $50, straight out of her wire before passing it on. Her bank never warned her. Her factory just saw a smaller number arrive. So she has to wire the missing $200. New transfer fee. Another delay. Factory still has not started production. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst skips the middleman chain entirely. The amount you send is the amount your supplier gets.
audience_feedback: $50,000 is big lah. My orders smaller. But the problem same. I had this last month, RM800 missing.
First line very strong. Supplier reply 'we only got 49,800.' That is the exact message I always get on WeChat.
'Middleman banks' I half understand. Like agent? OK can.
Why she have to wire AGAIN, new fee, more wait. That sentence hit me. Same story for me.
I trust this one more. Story feel real, not like ad. I might search.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst (internal: World Account + MYbank, OUR routing)
hook_options: $50,000 sent. $49,800 arrived. $200 gone. Here is the math.
Why is 8 to 15% of all wires to Chinese suppliers landing short?
Your wire to China hits 1 to 3 middleman banks. Each one keeps $15 to $50.
Your bank says zero fees deducted. Your supplier says $200 short. Both right.
$50K wire. 3 middleman banks. $50 each. Quick maths: your supplier gets $49,850.
variants.body: When you wire $50,000 to China through your bank, it passes through 1 to 3 middleman banks in the chain. Each one deducts $15 to $50 directly from your wire before passing it on. Your bank never tells you. Your supplier just sees a smaller number land. Quick maths: 3 middlemen at $50 each is $150 gone. Across a year of wires, that is real money. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst routes your CNY direct, no middleman chain, full amount lands with your supplier.
audience_feedback: I like 'here is the math'. Sound straight, no nonsense.
8 to 15% is what? Of which thing? I get lost on percent.
$15 to $50 each bank. OK I understand. Three banks take money. Like middle agent.
'A year of wires that is real money' good. Make me think about my whole year, not one wire.
'Search WorldFirst' I would maybe. But after my next coffee. Not now.

variants.wf_product: WorldFirst (internal: World Account + MYbank, OUR routing)
hook_options: Your bank told you the wire went through. The $200 that disappeared is not a bug.
Why is the bank not refunding the $200 your supplier never got?
Your supplier is not lying about the $200. Your bank is not lying about sending $50,000. Welcome to the chain.
Everyone tells you cross-border wires are reliable. 8 to 15% land short to China.
The $200 missing from your wire did not vanish. Somebody took it.
variants.body: Your bank moves your $50,000 by handing it to another bank, which hands it to another bank, until it reaches your supplier. That is how international wires actually work, even though banks rarely explain it. Each middleman bank takes a small fee, $15 to $50, out of the amount. By the time it lands, your supplier is short. Your bank shows the wire as complete because, from their side, it is. The chain is legal, documented, and standard. Nobody flags it because nobody loses money except you. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst goes direct, no chain to deduct from.
audience_feedback: 'Not a bug' good first line. Make me curious. Not a bug then what?
'Nobody loses money except you' OUCH. That hit me. True also.
Story explain step by step, bank to bank. Simple enough. I follow.
'Legal, documented, and standard' make me feel small. Like everyone know but me.
Look up WorldFirst, search WorldFirst, check out WorldFirst. All same lah. Tell me WHAT to do exactly.

videos.video_title: Why your wire arrived 3 days late and short $150

hook_options: A business owner sends a wire to China on a Monday. By Friday, her supplier is still chasing.
Why is your 2-day wire to China taking 5 days?
Day 1: wire sent. Day 5: supplier still asking where the money is. This is normal.
Your bank promised 2 business days. You are on day 5. Welcome to the China corridor.
Three middleman banks. One name spelling mismatch. Five days of delay.
variants.body: Her wire was supposed to clear in 2 business days. By Friday, day 5, her factory had still not seen the money. Two things were happening at once. First, middleman banks. Her wire was passing through 3 different banks in the middle, each taking a day. Second, the Chinese bank flagged the company name. Her wire said Shenzhen ABC Trading. The actual registered name was Shenzhen ABC Trading Co., Ltd. Three missing letters held the wire for manual review. Production got delayed by a week. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same-day CNY payments, no middlemen, no name mismatch holds.
audience_feedback: Monday to Friday. That is my life. I send Monday and ask my supplier every day where is the money.
The 'Co Ltd' part very good. I made this mistake before. Three letter missing also can hold money? Crazy.
Story too long for me maybe. I scroll at the middle. Got two problems in one video, confusing.
'Production delayed by a week' that is real pain. My customer also waiting.
After watching I think yeah maybe try WorldFirst. But same boring ending.

hook_options: Day 1 sender bank. Day 2 middleman A. Day 3 middleman B. Day 4 Chinese bank review. Day 5 supplier.
Why does a 2-day wire to China actually take 5 days, 8 out of 10 times?
5 to 10% of wires to China get returned for one reason: the company name has 3 letters missing.
Your bank quoted 2 business days. The chain has 4 banks. Quick maths: not 2 days.
$150 in middleman fees. 3 extra days waiting. One typo in the company name.
variants.body: A typical wire to China takes 5 days, not the 2 your bank quoted. Day 1: your bank processes. Day 2 and 3: middleman banks pass it along, each taking $15 to $50 in fees. Day 4: Chinese bank runs a compliance check, matching the name on the wire to the company registration. If you wrote Shenzhen ABC Trading but the registered name is Shenzhen ABC Trading Co., Ltd., it gets held for manual review. 5 to 10% of wires to China bounce back for this. Day 5: supplier finally sees it. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst verifies the supplier name upfront and sends direct.
audience_feedback: Day by day breakdown is good. I can see what is happening each day. Like a calendar.
'Compliance check' big word. I don't know. You mean they check the name? Just say that lah.
'Verifies supplier name upfront' OK this part useful. They check first so no problem after. Good.
5 to 10% bounce back? That mean 1 out of 10. That can happen to me. Scary.
I think this one I might search. Same-name-typo problem is the most real for me.

hook_options: Your wire was not delayed. It was on the slow path all along.
Why does your bank quote 2 days when the actual average is 5?
The wire is not lost. It is being held in a Chinese bank because the company name has a typo.
Everyone blames the supplier when wires arrive late. 80% of the time it is the chain.
Your bank told you 2 business days. They forgot to mention the other 3 banks in the chain.
variants.body: When your bank says 2 business days for a wire to China, they mean their own processing time. They do not count the middleman banks in the chain. A US to China wire usually hits 3 of them. They also do not count Chinese bank compliance, where your wire gets paused if the company name on it does not exactly match the registered name in China. Add it up and most wires actually take 5 days. The delay is built in. Your bank does not explain it. Your supplier waits. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst goes direct, same-day, no chain.
audience_feedback: 'US to China wire' but I am Malaysia not US. Feels not for me a bit.
'2 days they mean their own processing time'. AH SO THAT IS IT. Now I understand why bank always say 2 days but never 2 days.
Same-day is what I want. Tell me more about that, not the bank's process.
I don't trust 'goes direct, same-day, no chain' is real for everyone. Sound like ad-talk.
Probably not search. I scroll, see another video, forget.

card_title: Why paying your supplier in CNY actually wins

videos.video_title: USD feels safer. It costs you 2% more.

hook_options: A business owner pays her Chinese factory in USD. She thinks it is the safe choice. It costs her 2% extra.
Why is paying your supplier in USD costing you 2% more than paying in CNY?
$20,000 USD wire to China. $460 invisible loss. Two conversions, not one.
USD feels universal. It is also the most expensive currency to send to China.
Your supplier accepts USD. They also charge you a hidden fee for it.
variants.body: Her USD wire actually goes through two currency conversions, not one. First her bank converts her local currency into USD, takes 0.5 to 1.5%. Then her supplier's Chinese bank converts USD back into CNY on the other side, takes another 0.3 to 0.8%. That double conversion is invisible because it is baked into the rates, not the fee line. On a $20,000 order, she has quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars before anything ships. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY directly to your supplier. One conversion. Lower total cost. Same destination bank account.
audience_feedback: USD is what I always pay! She is me. First line make me sit up.
Two conversions, OK that mean my money changed twice. I get it.
'Baked into the rates' I don't really know what mean. The rate has hidden fee?
'Quietly given up 160 to 460 dollars' is real money for me. RM2000 about. That is one week food.
OK this one I would search. The USD habit is mine. Make me feel I been losing money long time.

hook_options: USD route: two currency conversions. CNY route: one. Total cost difference: 2%.
Why does a $20K USD wire cost $460 more than a $20K CNY wire to the same supplier?
$20,000 USD to China stacks two FX charges. Quick maths: $460 silent loss.
Your bank quotes USD wire as standard. The math says it costs 2x what a CNY wire costs.
0.5 to 1.5% on the send side. 0.3 to 0.8% on the receive side. That is your USD wire.
variants.body: When you send USD to a Chinese supplier, the money gets converted twice. Conversion one, your bank turns your local currency into USD, takes 0.5 to 1.5%. Conversion two, your supplier's bank turns that USD into CNY, takes 0.3 to 0.8%. Quick maths on a $20K order: that is up to $460 gone before your supplier even receives it. Pay in CNY directly: one conversion, 0.3 to 0.5% total. Same order, half the cost. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY direct, one conversion, transparent rate.
audience_feedback: Hook is just numbers. 'Two currency conversions, one, 2%.' Cold. I scroll if first line not story.
'FX charges' I don't really know. Foreign exchange? You mean change money fee?
$460 gone, I follow that part. Real number, real loss.
'Half the cost' OK that is clear. CNY costs half of USD.
I would search this one. But the numbers come too fast in the middle, give me less.

hook_options: Everyone says pay your supplier in USD. The math says pay them in CNY.
Why is USD considered the safe currency when it costs 2% extra in China?
Your bank pushes USD wires. Your supplier prefers CNY. The reason is the same: cost.
USD is the universal currency. In China, it is the universal extra fee.
The 'safe' currency to send your supplier is the one that gets converted twice on the way.
variants.body: USD looks safe because everyone uses it. That habit is exactly why it costs more. When you send USD to a Chinese supplier, the money gets converted twice. Your bank charges on the way out, the supplier's bank charges on the way in. Both conversions are invisible because they are baked into the rate. The CNY path avoids the second conversion entirely. One currency, one rate, one cost. Your supplier even prefers it: they get clean CNY instead of waiting 7 to 10 days for their bank to convert your USD. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY direct.
audience_feedback: 'Everyone says pay in USD. The math says CNY.' Good hook. Make me think I been doing it wrong.
'7 to 10 days for their bank to convert your USD' OH so my supplier also waits that long? I never know.
Easy to follow. Money in, money out, one fee instead of two.
'Your supplier even prefers it' good. Tell me my supplier wants this, that's a real reason.
Maybe search. But honest, I forget by tonight. I am tired.

videos.video_title: Why your supplier secretly wants CNY

hook_options: Your supplier accepts USD. They also quietly pad your invoice 2 to 5% to cover it.
Why does the same supplier quote you $10,000 in USD and 70,000 in CNY when 70,000 CNY is closer to $9,700?
Your supplier does not want your USD. They just do not say it out loud.
When your USD wire lands in China, your supplier waits 7 to 10 days to use it.
A factory owner in Guangzhou prefers CNY. The 2% USD markup on your invoice is why.
variants.body: Here is what happens when USD lands at a Chinese supplier's bank. They wait 7 to 10 days for the bank to clear the funds. They pay a receiving fee to convert the USD to CNY. And they carry the currency risk if the rate moves before settlement. None of that is your problem on paper. It becomes your problem on the next quote. They pad the USD price by 2 to 5% to protect themselves. Ask for the CNY price and that padding has no reason to exist. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst lets you pay your supplier in CNY directly, so you can ask for the CNY price and actually get it.
audience_feedback: 'Your supplier does not want your USD' is a strong line. New idea for me. I thought they like USD.
'Pad your invoice 2 to 5%' I had to read twice. 'Pad' is what? You mean add extra?
'Ask for the CNY price and watch what they quote' best line. That is a thing I can DO tomorrow.
'Carry the currency risk' is what? Lost me. Just say if rate change then they lose.
Yes I would search this one. Make me feel like I been outsmarted by my own supplier.

hook_options: Your supplier pads USD invoices 2 to 5%. Three reasons: wait, fee, rate risk.
Why is your supplier's USD price always 2 to 5% higher than their CNY price?
USD to a Chinese supplier: 7 to 10 day wait, plus receiving fee, plus rate risk. Quick maths: 2 to 5% markup.
Your supplier sees three downsides to USD. Each one gets priced into your invoice.
On a 100,000 CNY order: USD price is 5,000 CNY higher. That is the padding.
variants.body: Three reasons Chinese suppliers prefer CNY. One: speed. CNY lands in their account same-day. USD takes 7 to 10 days for their bank to clear and convert. Two: fee. Their bank charges them around 0.3 to 0.8% to convert your USD back to CNY. They eat that or push it to you. Three: rate risk. If the rate moves between when you quote and when you pay, they lose. All three get baked into the USD price as a 2 to 5% padding. Quick maths on a 100,000 CNY order: that is 5,000 CNY of padding gone. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY direct.
audience_feedback: 'Three reasons' OK my brain can take three. Not five not ten. Good.
'Rate risk' explain after, OK now I understand. Rate moves, they lose. Simple.
100,000 CNY is what in MYR? Don't know off top of head. Maybe RM60K. Big order for me.
'They eat that or push it to you' good casual line. Sound human.
I trust this one. Three reasons make it feel like real info not sales. Might search.

hook_options: Your supplier never asks for CNY because most foreign buyers cannot send it.
Why is your supplier accepting USD when they would rather have CNY?
Your supplier is not refusing CNY. They assumed you could not send it. Ask them.
Everyone tells you USD is the supplier-friendly currency. The supplier is just being polite.
Your supplier quotes USD because that is what you asked for. CNY was always available.
variants.body: Most Chinese suppliers prefer CNY. They just never bring it up because they assume foreign buyers cannot send it. So they quote USD, accept USD, and quietly pad the price by 2 to 5% to cover the conversion wait, the bank receiving fee, and the rate risk on their side. The conversation literally never happens. The moment a buyer asks for the CNY price, suppliers reveal the lower number. The padding had no reason to exist; it was insurance against a problem you can actually solve. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY directly to your supplier. Ask for the CNY price and watch what they quote.
audience_feedback: 'Your supplier never asks for CNY because most foreign buyers cannot send it' BIG ah-ha for me. Never thought of that.
'The supplier is just being polite' good. My supplier is very polite ya. Always say no problem to USD.
I get the message. Just ask for CNY price. Easy action.
'Insurance against a problem you can actually solve' nice line but a bit philosophy. I want practical.
Yes I would try. Tomorrow I WhatsApp my supplier and ask CNY price. Then maybe search WorldFirst.

videos.video_title: The math on USD vs CNY for a $20,000 supplier payment

hook_options: A business owner pays the same supplier $20,000 in USD. Her cost: 360 dollars more than paying in CNY.
Why does paying your supplier $20,000 in USD cost 360 dollars more than paying in CNY?
$20,000 USD wire to China. $360 quietly lost. Multiply by 12 orders: $4,320 a year.
Same supplier. Same order. USD costs $360 more than CNY. Here is exactly where it goes.
$20K to China. Same factory. Two routes. The math sits in someone else's pocket.
variants.body: Her order, $20,000. The USD route. Her bank converted her local currency into USD, taking 1%, that is 200 dollars. The supplier's bank converted USD into CNY, taking 0.5%, another 100 dollars hidden in the rate. Wire and middleman bank fees, around 60 dollars. Total leakage: 360 dollars and 4 days. The CNY route via WorldFirst. One conversion at 0.4%, 80 dollars, settled same-day. Difference: 280 dollars on one order. Run 12 orders a year and that is 3,360 dollars sitting in someone else's pocket. That is why you should use WorldFirst. Same order, less cost, faster delivery.
audience_feedback: '$360 more, here is exactly where it goes' OK now I want to know.
Numbers in middle a lot. 1%, 200, 0.5%, 100, 60, 280, 3360. My head spin.
But the year total $3,360 stick in my mind. That is real money. RM15,000 a year? Big.
'Sitting in someone else's pocket' GOOD line. Honest, a bit angry.
I might search. Year-cost is the part that work on me, not the per-order math.

hook_options: $20,000 USD wire to China. Breakdown: $200 send-side, $100 receive-side, $60 middleman. Total: $360.
Why does the same $20K supplier order cost $360 more in USD than in CNY?
Run the math on USD vs CNY for $20K. The gap is $280 per order. Quick maths: $3,360 a year.
Your USD wire to China carries 3 separate charges. Quick maths on $20K: $360.
$20K supplier order. USD route: $360 lost. CNY route: $80 cost. Same factory.
variants.body: Quick maths on a $20,000 supplier order. USD route. Send-side conversion at 1%: $200. Receive-side conversion at 0.5%: $100. Middleman bank fees in the chain: around $60. Total leakage: $360. Plus 3 to 5 days waiting. CNY route via WorldFirst. One conversion at 0.4%: $80. Settled same-day. Difference per order: $280. Run 12 orders a year, that is $3,360 sitting in someone else's pocket. Run 24 orders and the number doubles. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst's CNY rail strips out the second conversion and the middleman chain.
audience_feedback: Hook is just numbers and codes. Too dry for first second.
'Send-side, receive-side' I follow with effort. Make me think.
'24 orders the number doubles' clever way to make me think bigger.
'CNY rail' what is rail? Like train? Confusing.
I would search if I had a calculator in my head. Otherwise I scroll.

hook_options: Everyone runs the math on supplier discount. Nobody runs the math on the wire.
Why does negotiating $200 off the supplier price feel like a win when the wire quietly costs $360?
Your supplier negotiation just saved $200. Your USD wire just cost $360. Net result: minus $160.
You bargained the supplier down by 1%. The bank takes 2% on the way over.
Three days of negotiation vs three clicks on a different wire app.
variants.body: Most business owners spend hours negotiating with the supplier and zero minutes on the wire rail. On a $20,000 order, a hard-won 1% supplier discount saves $200. The default USD wire to China costs around $360 in invisible currency conversion fees. Net result: minus $160. You won the negotiation and still went backwards. Same order on a CNY rail costs around $80. That is a $280 swing per order, every order. Negotiating the wire pays more than negotiating the price. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY direct, one conversion, no middleman chain.
audience_feedback: OH this hit different. 'Everyone runs the math on discount but not the wire' that is exactly me. I bargain hard with my supplier.
'You won the negotiation and still went backwards' BEST LINE in the whole batch. I felt that.
'Three days of negotiation vs three clicks on a different wire app' make it feel easy. I like.
'Wire rail' again with the rail word. Just say 'how you send money'.
Yes this one I search. The discount angle is what gets me. I waste so much time bargaining.

card_title: The 3-letter code that decides if your wire is same-day

videos.video_title: The 3-letter code on every China wire (most operators never see it)

hook_options: A business owner sends $50,000 to China. Her supplier gets $49,850. The reason is a 3-letter code she never saw.
Why does one 3-letter setting on your wire decide if your supplier gets the full amount?
There is a 3-letter code on every wire to China. Your bank picks it for you and never tells you.
Your bank says no extra charge. Your supplier still got less. The code on the wire is why.
OUR. SHA. BEN. One of these is on every wire you send. Most business owners never see it.
variants.body: Here is what happened. On her wire form, there is a hidden setting with three options. OUR means the sender pays all the middleman bank fees so the supplier gets the full amount. SHA means the fees come out of the wire as it travels, so the supplier gets less. BEN means the supplier pays everything. Her bank picked SHA by default. That is why $150 disappeared on the way to China. Most business owners never see the code, never know it exists, and never get the option to change it. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY direct, no middlemen, no codes. Your supplier gets the full amount, same-day.
audience_feedback: '3-letter code I never saw' make me curious. What code? Now I want to know.
OUR, SHA, BEN. I have to remember three things. I will forget by tomorrow.
Wait, where is this code? On my bank form? I never see it before. Where to look?
'Your bank picked SHA by default' OK so bank do it without telling me. That is the bad part.
I want to check my next wire for this code. But honest, I am not techy. I might forget.

hook_options: 3 options. OUR, SHA, BEN. Cost difference: $30 to $150 per wire.
Why does a 3-letter code on your wire form change what your supplier receives by $150?
Your bank defaults to SHA. SHA means your supplier pays the chain fees. Quick maths: $50 short on average.
OUR: supplier gets full amount, you pay $30 to $75 more. SHA: supplier gets less, your bank's default.
$50K wire. SHA code. 3 middlemen at $50 each. Supplier gets $49,850.
variants.body: Every international wire has a 3-letter code that sets who pays the middleman bank fees in the chain. OUR: sender pays everything upfront, supplier gets the full amount, costs you $30 to $75 extra. SHA: fees come out of the wire as it travels, supplier gets less, your bank's default. BEN: supplier covers all fees, unpredictable arrival amount. On a $50K wire through 3 middleman banks at $50 each, SHA costs your supplier $150. Quick maths across 12 wires a year: $1,800 of supplier shortfalls. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst skips the middleman chain entirely.
audience_feedback: Three codes. OUR, SHA, BEN. Like SPM subjects. I memorise but I won't remember.
Information good. But too compact. I cannot follow OUR vs SHA vs BEN all in one go.
$1,800 a year shortfall. That is real. Sticks with me.
'Unpredictable arrival amount' for BEN, OK I understand. Money come can be different. Bad.
I trust the info but I would not search. Too much code talk for me.

hook_options: Your wire has a setting your bank picks for you. The default makes your supplier pay.
Why is the default setting on your China wire the one that costs your supplier money?
Your bank says 'shared fees'. The code on the wire actually says: supplier eats everything in the middle.
Everyone assumes a wire is a wire. There are three different wire types, and your bank picks the cheapest one for them.
The 3-letter code on your wire decides if your supplier complains tomorrow. You did not pick it.
variants.body: The 3-letter code on a wire is set by your bank when you initiate the transfer. The default at most banks is SHA, which sounds like 'shared' but actually means: you pay your bank's send fee, then your supplier eats every middleman bank deduction in the chain. The bank picks SHA because it is cheaper for them to process and gets your money out of their hands faster. Your supplier ends up short, you get a top-up request, and you wire again. The default is not a mistake. It is the cheapest option for the bank, not for you. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst skips the entire chain.
audience_feedback: 'Default makes your supplier pay' OK that is a problem I face. Top-up request very common.
'SHA sounds like shared but actually means supplier eats everything' GOOD reveal. I trusted the word 'shared'.
'Cheapest option for the bank, not for you' clear line. Bank put themselves first. I believe it.
I still cannot use this info on my own. I don't know where this code field is.
I might search WorldFirst because if they skip the whole problem, I don't need to remember the code.

card_title: Supplier said wire didn't arrive. Bank says it did.

videos.video_title: My bank says sent. My supplier says nothing. Both right.

hook_options: A business owner checks her bank app. Wire sent. She messages her supplier. Nothing arrived. Both are telling the truth.
Why does your bank say the wire is sent while your supplier says nothing has arrived?
Your bank: $50,000 sent on Monday. Your supplier on Friday: where is the money? Both right.
Sent does not mean arrived. The gap between the two is days long.
A business owner is staring at two screens. Bank says sent. WhatsApp says nothing. She is not crazy.
variants.body: Here is the gap. When her bank says sent, it means the wire left their system. It does not mean it arrived in her supplier's account. In between are 1 to 3 middleman banks, each holding the wire for up to a business day. There can be a currency conversion window of another 2 days. There might be a Chinese bank compliance check on the company name. Add a public holiday in China or her own region and a wire can sit silently for a full week, with no notification, no update, no tracking number that means anything. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst's CNY payments land same-day, with a tracking number you can actually check.
audience_feedback: OMG first line is my life. Bank app says sent. WhatsApp says nothing. Every single time.
'She is not crazy' funny and true. I feel crazy every time also.
Public holiday part very real. CNY holiday is the worst. Two weeks no movement.
'Tracking number you can actually check' GREAT. Like DHL but for money. That I want.
Yes I search. This one nail the pain. I am ready for a fix.

hook_options: Sent on Monday. Arrives Friday. 4 days in the middleman chain.
Why is your wire to China still sitting between two banks 4 days after it was sent?
Bank says sent. Supplier says nothing. Gap: 1 to 5 business days. Cause: middleman chain plus compliance.
Your wire to China hits 3 middleman banks. Each one holds it for up to 24 hours. Quick maths: 3 days minimum.
Day 1 sent. Day 4 still in compliance review. Day 5 lands. Day 6 you get the angry message.
variants.body: From 'sent' to 'arrived', a typical China wire goes through 1 to 3 middleman banks, each holding the wire for up to 24 hours. Plus currency conversion, another 2 business days. Plus Chinese bank compliance review on the supplier's name, another 1 to 3 days if there is any mismatch. Plus any local public holiday on either side. Quick maths: 4 to 7 working days from sent to arrived is normal for a US to China wire. Your bank does not show you any of this, because their job ended when the wire left their system. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst goes direct on the same-day rail.
audience_feedback: 'Sent Monday. Arrives Friday.' Yes my life. Hook is good.
Plus plus plus, so many things to add up. Middleman, conversion, compliance, holiday. I lose track.
'Your bank does not show you any of this because their job ended' YES this is the part that make me angry.
Again US to China. I am Malaysia. Wonder if same for me.
I would maybe search. But the breakdown of days is a bit much. Story version was better.

hook_options: Your bank is not lying. Your supplier is not lying. The wire is just nowhere yet.
Why does 'sent' from your bank and 'arrived' for your supplier mean two different things?
Everyone assumes a wire is a single event. It is 4 separate handoffs that nobody coordinates.
Your bank's job ended the moment they handed your wire to someone else. The rest is somebody else's problem.
'Sent' is not a confirmation of arrival. It is a confirmation that your bank's part is done.
variants.body: Your bank uses 'sent' to mean the wire left their system. Your supplier uses 'arrived' to mean it appeared in their account. Between those two points are 3 or 4 separate banks, each running their own queue, each accountable only to themselves. Nobody is tracking the wire end to end. There is no Amazon-style tracking page. When your bank says sent and your supplier says nothing, both are accurate descriptions of their own boundary. The actual money is somewhere in the middle, with nobody watching it. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst owns the rail end to end, so sent and arrived mean the same day.
audience_feedback: 'The wire is just nowhere yet.' Great line. Make me laugh. Sad laugh.
'No Amazon-style tracking page' OH that explain it well. I order Shopee, I know where my parcel. Why my money no tracking?
'Money is somewhere in the middle with nobody watching it' SCARY. That is my money lah.
'Owns the rail end to end' I think mean they do everything themselves. OK good.
I would search. Amazon-tracking line is what stuck with me. Make it feel modern.

card_title: Why your CNY payment sometimes arrives in USD

videos.video_title: 3 places where your CNY can become USD

hook_options: A business owner sends CNY to her Chinese factory. The supplier opens the receipt and sees USD. Now she is paying for two conversions.
Why does your CNY wire to China sometimes arrive in USD?
You wired CNY. Your supplier received USD. Three different banks in the chain can do that.
Your bank confirmation says CNY sent. Your supplier got USD. Both happened.
Three different places in the chain can turn your CNY into USD without telling you.
variants.body: Here is how that happens. Place one, her own bank, decided to convert her local currency to USD before sending, because USD was cheaper for them to route. Place two, a middleman bank in the chain, only handles USD, so any CNY passing through got converted there. Place three, her supplier's bank could not accept CNY directly, so it converted the arriving currency to USD or back to CNY in another step. Each conversion has a fee, hidden in the rate. By the time her supplier sees the money, she has paid for two conversions when she thought she was paying for none. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY directly to your supplier's bank. One currency. One conversion. No surprises.
audience_feedback: Wait wait, I send CNY but supplier got USD? That is even possible? Hook make me confused but also curious.
Three places. My head not big enough. By place three I forgot place one.
'Hidden in the rate' again. Tell me where to see it. I don't trust 'hidden' without showing me.
Honest I never had this problem. I always send USD. So this video not for my situation.
I might not search. Too many cases in one video. Story not specific enough to me.

hook_options: 3 places your CNY can flip to USD. Each one charges 0.3 to 1.5%.
Why is your CNY wire showing up as USD on your supplier's receipt?
CNY sent. USD received. Each conversion in between costs 0.3 to 1.5%. Quick maths on $50K: $750 to $1,500.
Your bank, middleman bank, supplier's bank. Each one can flip your CNY to USD. That is 3 chances to add a fee.
Three places, three currency conversions, three fees. Same $50K. Same supplier.
variants.body: Three places where your CNY wire can get switched to USD. One: your bank converts your local currency to USD on the send side because that is cheaper for them, takes 0.5 to 1.5%. Two: a middleman bank in the chain only handles USD, so any CNY passing through gets converted there. Three: the supplier's bank cannot accept CNY directly, so it converts on arrival, takes another 0.3 to 0.8%. Quick maths on a $50,000 wire with two of those conversions: $400 to $1,150 in hidden conversion costs. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY directly to the supplier's bank, one conversion, transparent rate.
audience_feedback: '3 chances to add a fee' the phrase I understand. Three chances to lose money.
Lot of percent. 0.3 to 1.5%, 0.5 to 1.5%, 0.3 to 0.8%. They all blur together.
$50K is big amount. Make me feel maybe this not for me. My wires more $5K to $10K.
'Transparent rate' OK I get. You see the rate clearly. Good word.
Honest I scroll. Topic too narrow for me. CNY mix USD never happened to me yet.

hook_options: You sent CNY because it was supposed to be the clean option. The chain undid that on the way.
Why does selecting CNY on your wire form not actually mean CNY arrives?
Your CNY wire is not guaranteed to stay CNY. The chain decides what arrives.
Everyone assumes selecting CNY on your wire ticket means CNY lands. The chain has other plans.
CNY in. USD out. Your bank, middleman bank, and supplier's bank all had a say.
variants.body: Selecting CNY on your wire form does not guarantee CNY lands at your supplier. The wire passes through 2 or 3 separate banks, and any of them can convert the currency for its own reasons. Your bank might convert to USD because it does not hold CNY. A middleman bank in the chain might only handle USD. Your supplier's bank might convert on arrival because it cannot accept CNY directly. Each one is making the decision that is cheapest or simplest for them, not for you. The 'CNY' you selected was a request, not a guarantee. That is why you should use WorldFirst. WorldFirst sends CNY directly, no chain to override it.
audience_feedback: 'The CNY you selected was a request, not a guarantee' OH. So I tick CNY but they decide. That make me feel small.
'The chain has other plans' nice line. Like a movie title. Make me listen.
I follow the logic. Three banks, three decisions. Each one for themselves not me.
Still feel like a problem I haven't had. I send USD usually. So why I care?
Maybe scroll. Information is good but the topic is for someone who does CNY already. Not me yet.
